meta.title: PyQuest — Learn Python by Playing
meta.tagline: Minecraft x Duolingo x Khan Academy, for Python

worlds.name: Python Village
worlds.blurb: A cozy village where you meet Py the snake and learn to store treasures in variables.

lessons.title: The Treasure Chests (Variables)
lessons.story: Py the snake found three magic chests! Each chest can hold ONE treasure and has a name written on it. In Python, a chest is called a *variable*. You put a treasure inside with the = sign.
explain: A **variable** is a labeled box that stores a value.
You create one like this:  `gold = 100`
The name is on the left, the value is on the right, `=` puts it in.
You can look inside any time by printing it:  `print(gold)`

playground.prompt: Make a variable called `score` equal to 7, then print it.
playground.starter: # put 7 into a chest named score

quiz.q: Which line stores the number 5 in a box named coins?
quiz.hint: The name goes on the LEFT of the = sign.

quiz.q: How do you show the value of coins on screen?
quiz.hint: Python uses a friendly word that means 'put on screen'.

lessons.title: Words & Numbers (Data Types)
lessons.story: Some chests hold NUMBERS (like coins) and some hold WORDS (like names). Py learns that Python treats them differently — you can add numbers, and you can glue words together!
explain: **Numbers**: `42`, `3.14`  — you can do math with them.
**Strings** (words): `"hello"` — always wrapped in quotes.
**Booleans**: `True` or `False` — yes/no answers.
Glue strings with +:  `"Py" + "thon"` makes `"Python"`.
lessons.example:
name = "Py"
level = 3
brave = True
print(name + " the snake")
print(level + 1)

playground.prompt: Create name = "Mia" and age = 9, then print:  Mia is 9   (hint: use str(age) to glue a number to words).

quiz.q: What is True or False called in Python?
quiz.hint: It starts with 'b' and means yes/no.

lessons.title: Boss Battle: The Greeting Gate 🐉
lessons.story: A friendly dragon guards the village gate. To pass, you must teach the gate to greet ANY hero by name and tell them their starting score!
explain: Combine everything: variables, strings, numbers, and print().
Use str() to put a number inside a sentence.
lessons.example:
hero = "Sam"
score = 0
print("Welcome " + hero + "! Score: " + str(score))

playground.prompt: Set hero = "Leo" and score = 10. Print exactly:  Welcome Leo! Score: 10

options: To glue a number into a word sentence
quiz.hint: You can't glue a number directly to words with +.

worlds.name: Decision Caves
worlds.blurb: Dark caves with forking paths. Choose wisely using if / elif / else.

lessons.title: Which Path? (if / else)
lessons.story: Two tunnels! One has treasure, one has a sleeping bat. Py uses an `if` to decide which way to go based on a torch's brightness.
explain: `if` runs code ONLY when something is True.
`else` runs when it's False.
Compare with `>`, `<`, `==` (equals), `!=` (not equal).
Don't forget the colon `:` and the indent (4 spaces)!

playground.prompt: Set coins = 3. If coins is greater than 5 print "rich", otherwise print "keep going".

quiz.hint: One = puts in, two == asks a question.

worlds.name: Loop Mountain
worlds.blurb: Climb the mountain by repeating magic steps with loops.

lessons.title: Magic Repeat (for loop)
lessons.story: To climb the mountain, Py must shout 'step!' five times. Instead of typing it 5 times, a `for` loop does the repeating for you!
explain: `for i in range(5):` repeats 5 times.
`range(5)` gives the numbers 0,1,2,3,4.
The indented lines are what gets repeated.

playground.prompt: Use a for loop to print the numbers 1, 2, 3 (each on its own line). Hint: range(1, 4).

quiz.q: How many times does range(4) repeat?

worlds.name: Function Castle
worlds.blurb: Build magical machines (functions) that do a job whenever you call them.

lessons.title: Build a Machine (def)
lessons.story: The castle wizard teaches Py to build a spell machine: write the spell once, then cast it anytime by name.
explain: `def greet():` creates a function (a reusable machine).
Call it by writing its name with ():  `greet()`
Parameters are inputs:  `def greet(name):`
`return` sends an answer back out.

playground.prompt: Write a function double(n) that returns n times 2, then print double(5).

quiz.q: What keyword sends a value back from a function?

worlds.name: Data Forest
worlds.blurb: A forest of collections: lists, tuples, dictionaries and sets.

lessons.title: The Backpack (Lists)
lessons.story: Py's backpack holds many items in order. A Python list is exactly that — a backpack you can add to, take from, and count.
explain: A list holds many values:  `bag = ["apple", "rope", "key"]`
Get the first item with `bag[0]` (counting starts at 0!).
Add with `bag.append("map")`.
Count with `len(bag)`.

playground.prompt: Make a list nums = [10, 20, 30] and print the LAST item using nums[-1].

worlds.name: Class Kingdom
worlds.blurb: Design your own creatures with classes and objects (OOP).

lessons.title: Create a Creature (Classes)
lessons.story: In the Class Kingdom, Py learns to design a blueprint for creatures. One blueprint (class) can make many creatures (objects)!
explain: A `class` is a blueprint.  `class Pet:`
`__init__` sets up each new pet with its own data.
`self` means 'this particular pet'.
Make an object:  `p = Pet("Rex")`

playground.prompt: Make a class Hero with __init__(self, name) and a method shout(self) that returns name + "!!!". Create Hero("Zoe") and print zoe.shout().

worlds.name: Async Nebula
worlds.blurb: Master advanced powers: decorators, generators, async, and testing.

lessons.title: Lazy Magic (Lambda & map)
lessons.story: Out in the nebula, Py discovers tiny one-line spells called lambdas, and a power called map that casts a spell on every star at once.
explain: A `lambda` is a tiny function:  `double = lambda x: x * 2`
`map` applies a function to every item:  `map(double, [1,2,3])`
Wrap with `list(...)` to see the result.

playground.prompt: Use map with a lambda to triple every number in [1, 2, 3]. Print the list.  Expected: [3, 6, 9]

quiz.hint: It's a function with no name, written in one line.

worlds.name: Builder's Realm
worlds.blurb: Build real things: APIs, data structures, algorithms, and your final game.

lessons.title: Capstone: The Number Genie 🎰
lessons.story: Final challenge! Build a Number Guessing helper: the Genie thinks of 7. Write code that checks a guess and prints 'too low', 'too high', or 'correct!'.
explain: Combine variables, comparison, and if/elif/else.
This is a real mini-project — the kind you can grow into a full game!

playground.prompt: secret = 7. Set guess = 9. Print 'too high' (use if/elif/else like the example).

options: to check another condition if the first was False
quiz.hint: elif = 'else, if this other thing is true'.
